title: Print, Comments, and First Errors
summary: Use print statements and comments while learning how syntax errors appear.

sketchBlocks.title: Printing text to the output panel
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Python uses `print()` to show output.

When you write text, put it inside quotes so Python knows it is a string.

```python
print("Hello")
print("Welcome to Python")
```

If you click Run, the output panel shows:

```text
Hello
Welcome to Python
```

Step by step:
- `print("Hello")` displays `Hello`
- `print("Welcome to Python")` displays `Welcome to Python`
- Each `print()` starts a new line in the output panel

Worked example:

```python
print("My name is Ava")
print("I like coding")
```

Output:

```text
My name is Ava
I like coding
```

Try it yourself: change one of the messages, click Run, and check how the output panel changes.

sketchBlocks.title: Comments are notes for people, not output
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
A comment starts with `#`. Python ignores comments when the program runs.

```python
# This is a comment
print("Hi")
# This line explains the next message
print("Bye")
```

Output:

```text
Hi
Bye
```

Line by line:
- `# This is a comment` is ignored by Python
- `print("Hi")` runs and shows `Hi`
- `# This line explains the next message` is also ignored
- `print("Bye")` runs and shows `Bye`

Comments are useful for reminders and explanations.

Worked example:

```python
# Introduce the program
print("Student profile")
# Show a hobby
print("Hobby: drawing")
```

Try this: add your own comment above a `print()` line. Click Run and notice that the comment does not appear in the output panel.

sketchBlocks.title: What a first syntax error looks like
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
A syntax error happens when Python cannot read your code because the structure is broken.

A common beginner mistake is forgetting a quote or a parenthesis.

```python
print("Hello"
```

This causes an error because the closing `)` is missing.

Another example:

```python
print("Hello)
```

This causes an error because the string is missing its closing quote.

Correct version:

```python
print("Hello")
```

Step by step:
- `print(` starts the print call
- `"Hello"` is the text to display
- `)` closes the print call

Your turn: type a correct `print()` line in the code editor, click Run, and check the output panel. Then carefully remove one quote and see how the error changes.

quizDraft.title: What print does
options: It displays Hello in the output panel.
It turns Hello into a comment.
It creates a syntax error every time.
It hides Hello from the program.
quizDraft.hint: Think about what appears in the output panel after you click Run.

help.concept: `print()` sends a value to the output panel so you can see program output.
help.hint_1: This line is not a comment and not an error. It runs and shows text.
help.hint_2: Look for the choice that describes displaying `Hello` in the output panel.

quizDraft.title: Recognizing a comment
quizDraft.prompt: Which line is a Python comment?
quizDraft.hint: A Python comment starts with a special symbol.

help.concept: In Python, a comment begins with `#` and is ignored when the code runs.
help.hint_1: Only one option starts with the comment marker used in Python.
help.hint_2: Choose the line that begins with `#`, not a `print()` call.

quizDraft.title: Valid print statements
quizDraft.prompt: Choose all lines that are valid Python print statements.
quizDraft.hint: A valid print statement here needs `print`, parentheses, and correctly closed quotes.

help.concept: A basic Python `print()` call needs matching parentheses and matching quotes around text.
help.hint_1: Check each option for two things: are the quotes closed, and is the closing `)` present?
help.hint_2: Two options are complete and balanced; the others are missing a quote or a parenthesis.

quizDraft.title: How comments behave
quizDraft.prompt: Choose all true statements about Python comments.
options: They are shown in the output panel as normal output.
Python ignores them when running the program.

quizDraft.title: Order the program lines
quizDraft.prompt: Arrange these lines to make a simple program that shows a title and then a greeting.
quizDraft.hint: The comment can go first, and the title should print before the greeting.

help.concept: Python runs top to bottom, so the order of lines controls the order of output.
help.hint_1: Place the comment before the code it describes.
help.hint_2: The line printing `Title` should come before the line printing `Hello`.

quizDraft.title: Build a correct print call
quizDraft.prompt: Put the tokens in order to form a valid Python line that prints Hi.
quizDraft.hint: Read each piece and arrange them in the order the statement should be understood.

help.concept: The pieces should form a valid statement in a logical order.
help.hint_1: Start with the piece that introduces the idea or action.
help.hint_2: Place dependent pieces after the part they describe or complete.

quizDraft.title: Fill the comment marker
quizDraft.prompt: Complete the line so it becomes a Python comment.
quizDraft.hint: Read the sentence around the blank and decide what role the missing word plays.

help.concept: The blank should be filled with the term that makes the statement accurate.
help.hint_1: Look at the words before and after the blank to infer what kind of term is needed.
help.hint_2: Pick the choice that completes the statement with the clearest meaning.

quizDraft.hint: Read the sentence around the blank and decide what role the missing word plays.

help.concept: The blank should be filled with the term that makes the statement accurate.

quizDraft.title: Print a welcome message
quizDraft.prompt: Write Python code that prints exactly `Welcome to Python` on one line.
quizDraft.hint: Use one `print()` call with the text inside quotes.

help.concept: To display text, use `print()` with a string literal in quotes.
help.hint_1: The output should be one line only, with the exact capitalization shown.
help.hint_2: Put the message inside quotes and pass it to `print()`.

quizDraft.title: Print two lines
quizDraft.prompt: Write Python code that prints `Name: Alex` on the first line and `City: Lima` on the second line.
quizDraft.hint: Read the task "Print two lines" and identify the required result.

help.concept: This coding exercise checks whether your code produces the requested result for: "Print two lines".
help.hint_1: Use the statement or expression that matches the required behavior.
help.hint_2: Run the code and compare the output panel with the expected result.

quizDraft.title: Use a comment and print
quizDraft.prompt: Write Python code with a comment on the first line and a `print()` statement on the second line that prints exactly `Ready`.
quizDraft.starterCode:

# Then write the print line below

quizDraft.title: Fix the syntax error
quizDraft.prompt: The starter code has a syntax error. Fix it so the program prints exactly `Hello`.
quizDraft.hint: Check whether the `print()` call is fully closed.

help.concept: A missing parenthesis in a function call causes a syntax error because Python cannot finish reading the statement.
help.hint_1: The text is already correct; the structure around it is incomplete.
help.hint_2: Make sure the `print(` line ends with the closing symbol that matches the opening `(`.

quizDraft.title: Build a tiny introduction
quizDraft.prompt:
Write Python code that prints these three lines exactly:
`About me`
`Name: Sam`
`Favorite color: Green`
quizDraft.solutionCode:
print("About me")
print("Name: Sam")
print("Favorite color: Green")

tests.stdout:
About me
Name: Sam
Favorite color: Green
